articles.author: Munir Ahmed, Sam Metz
articles.description: The standoff between the United States and Iran is deepening as the U.S. has declared it has blockaded the Strait of Hormuz. Iran has threatened to strike targets across the region. Pakistan says it is racing to bring the sides together for more talks. Last week’s ceasefire appeared to hold on Tuesday. But the standoff set up an extraordinary showdown that risked reigniting hostilities. Talks aimed at permanently ending the conflict failed to produce an agreement last weekend. But Pakistani officials told The Associated Press that they had proposed a second round in the coming days.
articles.title: Efforts underway for second round of US-Iran talks

articles.author: Lorian Belanger
articles.description: Pakistani officials said Tuesday that Islamabad has proposed a second round of talks to the U.S. and Iran, while U.S. Vice President JD Vance said on the Fox News Channel that negotiations with Iran “did make some progress.”
articles.title: Live updates: Pakistan proposes new US-Iran talks as Vance and Trump hint at progress

articles.author: Josef Federman
articles.description: U.S. President Donald Trump has escalated tensions with Iran by announcing a blockade of all Iranian ports. He made the announcement on Monday after 21-hour-long U.S.-Iran ceasefire talks in Pakistan ended without agreement. The blockade aims to pressure Iran to open the critical and strategic Strait of Hormuz and accept a deal to end the war. Iran has responded with threats against other ports in the region, raising concerns that the ceasefire could collapse. Trump claims Iran still wants a deal, but both sides remain entrenched. Pakistan's foreign minister has expressed hope for renewed dialogue.
articles.title: Trump escalates Iran tensions with US military blockade

articles.author: Kareem Chehayeb, Matthew Lee
articles.description: Lebanon and Israel are holding direct diplomatic talks for the first time in decades. The meeting is set for Tuesday in Washington following more than a month of conflict between Israel and Lebanese millitant group Hezbollah. U.S. Secretary of State Marco Rubio will participate alongside the Israeli and Lebanese ambassadors. The Lebanese government hopes the talks will lead to an end to the war, which has killed over 2,000 people in Lebanon. Hezbollah and critics are skeptical, believing Lebanon lacks leverage. Israel has continued its invasion of southern Lebanon, aiming to create what it calls a security zone. Talks aim to ensure long-term security on Israel’s northern border.
articles.title: Lebanon and Israel to hold direct diplomatic talks

articles.author: Bill Barrow
articles.description: Pope Leo XIV, a studious and soft-spoken cleric, and Donald Trump, an unapologetically bellicose and pugilistic politician, have long been on a rhetorical collision course. Now their disagreement over the war in Iran has escalated in spectacular fashion, and their comments show how differently each see the conflict and its impact. On social media, Trump said Leo was “Weak” and captive to the “Radical Left,” even suggesting that Leo somehow owed his position to Trump. The pope has declared Trump’s threats toward Iran “truly unacceptable” and pointed his flock to Biblical text and church doctrine on war and peace, explaining that his purpose is not about Trump at all. It’s an unusual spectacle involving the world’s two biggest megaphones.
articles.title: Trump and Pope Leo: Behind their disagreement over Iran war

articles.author: David Crary, Peter Smith, Steve Peoples
articles.description: A majority of U.S. Catholic voters supported Donald Trump in his 2024 presidential victory. However, there is dismay in Catholic ranks over Trump’s verbal assault on Pope Leo XIV, the first American pope. Leo says he is sharing a Gospel message, not directly attacking Trump. Criticism of Trump came from Archbishop Paul Coakley and Bishop Robert Barron, who called Trump’s remarks “entirely inappropriate.” Dismay extended to conservative Christian evangelicals over another issue. Many were appalled by Trump posting an image seeming to depict him as a Christ-like savior. By midday Monday, the image had been taken down from Truth Social.
articles.title: Many US Catholics dismayed by Trump's verbal attack on Pope Leo

articles.author: Michael R. Blood
articles.description: After the dramatic downfall of Democratic U.S. Rep. Eric Swalwell, the race for California governor is moving on. Once a leading candidate to replace outgoing Democratic Gov. Gavin Newsom, Swalwell suspended his campaign and then announced he would resign from Congress following allegations that he sexually assaulted a woman. A lengthy lineup of remaining candidates is scheduled to gather Tuesday in Sacramento, their first public appearance together since Swalwell’s departure reshaped the wide-open contest. Swalwell’s exit represents an opportunity for his former rivals to pick up support just weeks before mail ballots go to voters in early May.
articles.title: Race for California governor moves on after Swalwell campaign collapses

articles.author: Kevin Freking
articles.description: Republican Rep. Tony Gonzales of Texas says he will retire from Congress amid bipartisan calls to expel him. Gonzales had already said he would not seek reelection after admitting to an affair with a staff member who later died by suicide. House Republican leaders had called on the three-term Gonzales to not seek reelection.
articles.title: Republican Rep. Tony Gonzales of Texas says he will retire

articles.author: Brian Witte
articles.description: A proposal for a new Maryland congressional map has officially died with the conclusion of the state's legislative session. Maryland's session ended late Monday, but mid-decade redistricting efforts are set to continue next week in Florida and Virginia. A new Maryland map with the goal of ousting the state’s lone Republican U.S. House member in a heavily blue state was supported by Democratic Gov. Wes Moore, who is often mentioned by pundits as a potential 2028 presidential candidate. While the map was approved in the Maryland House, the state Senate never voted on it due to concerns it could backfire and cost Democrats a seat.
articles.title: Maryland lawmakers reject effort to redraw congressional map to boost Democrats

articles.author: Alia Wong
articles.description: A growing number of working-age people in the U.S. have attended college without earning a degree — a group that often has student loans to pay but without the credential to boost their earnings. Often referred to as “stopouts,” the group includes about 38 million people. In recent years, however, colleges and local governments have gotten better at helping them get back on track. The number of stopouts reenrolling has been on the rise, reaching more than 1 million in the 2023-2024 school year, a 7% increase from the year before, according to enrollment data.
articles.title: Dropped out? Colleges are helping students reenroll to finish degrees
